senses.definition: a Latin term meaning 'for prosecution,' used to refer to a writ or order to deliver a person for prosecution in legal proceedings
examples: The judge issued an ad prosequendum to produce the prisoner for trial.
An ad prosequendum may be used in habeas corpus cases.

senses.definition: a legal term meaning the act of following up or prosecuting; the formal process to continue or discontinue a case
examples: The prosecutor filed a prosequi to pursue the charges.
Nolle prosequi means the prosecutor has decided not to pursue the case.

senses.definition: a Latin phrase meaning 'it proceeds' or 'it follows,' used in Latin legal or rhetorical contexts to indicate continuation
examples: In traditional legal Latin usage, the prosequitur indicated the matter continues.
The clause prosequitur was employed to show sequence.

senses.definition: a name form of the goddess Proserpina; one who proserps (historical/variant spelling) relates to the mythic figure linked to spring and the underworld
examples: In Roman myth, Proser is another name for Proserpina.
The poet wrote of Proser in classical allusions.

senses.definition: the Roman goddess of the Underworld and springtime; equivalent to the Greek Persephone
examples: Proserpina is the wife of Dis in Roman myth.
Her story explains the changing seasons.

senses.definition: a genus name in botany, Proserpinaca; a flowering aquatic plant commonly called mermaid weed
examples: Proserpinaca is an aquatic plant found in wetlands.
Botanists study Proserpinaca species for freshwater habitats.

senses.definition: plural of 'prosere' (rare or dialectal) referring to prosers or to things that are prosers; may appear in scholarly or archaic contexts
examples: In some genealogical records, the term prosers appears.
The text uses prosers to denote earlier items.

senses.definition: plural form of prose; used to refer to prose texts or prose writing in general
examples: The proses of the anthology vary in tone.
He studies proses from the 19th century.

senses.definition: an anatomical term likely referring to a bone associated with the ethmoid region; very rare in common usage
examples: The radiologist noted a malformation near the prosethmoid area.
Anatomy texts mention the prosethmoid region in specialized contexts.

senses.definition: plural form of proseuchum? likely a philological or transliteration variant of 'proseucha' meaning a Jewish prayer hall; context unclear; may be misentry
examples: Historical texts sometimes list proseuchae as prayer places.

senses.definition: singular form of a prayer place; variant spelling related to proseucha; indicates a prayer house in some historical/linguistic contexts
examples: The manuscript mentions a proseuche as a place for prayer.

senses.definition: comparative form of 'prosy'? possibly a misspelling or rare form; not standard in contemporary English
examples: The term prosier does not appear in common dictionaries.

senses.definition: superlative form of 'prosy' meaning most prosaic or dull in style
examples: The prose style grew prosiest as the chapter wore on.

senses.definition: the act or process of turning something into prose; the making of prose, often used in didactic or translation contexts
examples: Prosification can make poetry easier to read in prose form.
The translator discussed prosification strategies.

senses.definition: made into prose; converted from verse or other form into prose
examples: The poem was prosified for the reader.
He prosified the ballad to suit a modern audience.

senses.definition: one who prosifies; a person who turns poetry or other text into prose
examples: An expert prosifier adapted the verses into prose.

senses.definition: turns text into prose; converts verse or other forms into prose
examples: The editor prosifies the poetry for readability.
She prosifies old ballads for the anthology.

senses.definition: to turn something into prose; to write prose instead of poetry or verse
examples: Authors sometimes prosify their fragments.
The editor asked him to prosify the poem.

senses.definition: the act of turning something into prose
examples: Prosifying poetry can alter its rhythm.
They are prosifying the verse for classrooms.

senses.definition: plural of 'prosiliency'—often rare or theoretical term relating to proficiency or preparedness; not common in modern usage
examples: The text discusses various prosiliencies in language learning.

senses.definition: an uncommon or archaic term meaning readiness or proficiency
examples: Her prosiliency in solving problems impressed the panel.

senses.definition: adjective form meaning having or showing prosperity or resilience or strength; not common in modern usage
examples: The prosilient society recovered quickly after the disaster.

senses.definition: in a prosilient manner; in a way that shows resilience or prosperity
examples: The region recovered prosiliently after the flood.

senses.definition: a mineral or mineral-like substance; possibly a proper mineral name; not widely used in everyday vocabulary
examples: The geologist identified a sample as prosilverite.
